text: A combination of simulated live high-train low (LHTL) altitude exposure and natural moderate altitude training camps improves competitive race performance in elite middle-distance runners by approximately 1.9%.
An effective LHTL protocol involves approximately 44 nights of simulated altitude exposure at ~2850 m before transitioning to natural altitude training camps.
Natural moderate altitude training camps of 7-10 days at 1700-2200 m can serve as an effective follow-up to extended simulated LHTL exposure before competition.
Cumulative altitude exposure combining simulated and natural altitude modalities can be used sequentially to stimulate performance-enhancing physiological adaptations.
Multiple short natural altitude training camps (4 camps of 7-10 days) following an extended LHTL block provide additional adaptive stimulus beyond simulated altitude alone.
